Chapter 1 · Introduction

What Is an
AI Agent?

An intuitive, in-depth journey through the anatomy of AI Agents — from the fundamentals of Large Language Models to autonomous, multi-modal systems.

↓ SCROLL
Interactive Primer

How Does a Machine
Actually Learn?

Before we dive into AI Agents, let's see the fundamentals in action. Walk through how an ML model reads documents, counts words, builds a probability matrix, and uses it to predict topics.

Step 1 of 5

📚 Step 1 — Feed It Some Documents

Every ML model starts the same way: it reads a bunch of examples. These are called training data. Below are six documents, each belonging to a topic — Food, Tech, or Sports.

💡 Real-World Analogy

Think of a kid learning what a "dog" is. You don't give a dictionary definition — you show them lots of dogs. Eventually their brain builds a mental picture of "dog-ness."

🔍 Step 2 — Count the Words

The model can't understand meaning. It just counts how often each word appears in each topic's documents.

WordCountFrequency
💡 Real-World Analogy

Imagine figuring out what magazine someone reads by counting how many times "recipe" shows up versus "quarterback."

📊 Step 3 — Build the Probability Matrix

Divide each word's count by the total words in that topic. The result is a probability. Hover any cell to see the calculation.

Hover a cell above…
💡 Real-World Analogy

It's like a scorecard. Each word gets a score for every topic. "Pizza" scores high for Food but low for Tech.

🧮 Step 4 — How Prediction Works

For a new sentence, look up every word's probability per topic and multiply them together. Highest score wins.

INPUT
New Sentence
→
LOOK UP
Word
Probabilities
→
MULTIPLY
Scores
Together
→
OUTPUT
Highest Score
= Prediction
💡 Real-World Analogy

Each word "votes" for a topic. Pizza votes Food. Laptop votes Tech. Most votes wins.

🚀 Step 5 — Try It Yourself

Type a sentence and watch the model predict its topic in real time.

💬 Try: "I love tacos and pizza" · "The new laptop is amazing" · "The soccer game was incredible"

Prediction

Start typing to see the model predict…

💡 Under the hood

The model scans every word, looks each up in the matrix, multiplies scores per topic, and shows the winner.

✅ What Did We Learn?

1

Training data is labeled examples the model reads.

2

Word counting turns text into numbers.

3

Dividing counts creates a probability matrix — the model's "brain."

4

New sentences are classified by multiplying probabilities.

5

This is called Naïve Bayes — one of the simplest ML classifiers.

Interactive Deep-Dive

Retrieval-Augmented
Generation (RAG)

LLMs can only use what's in their prompt. RAG solves this by retrieving relevant documents from a knowledge base and injecting them into the prompt before the LLM generates an answer. Walk through each function that makes this work.

Step 1
Tokenize
→
Step 2
Vectorize
→
Step 3
Compare
→
Step 4
Retrieve
→
Step 5
Augment
→
Step 6
Generate
Step 1 of 6

Step 1 — Tokenize tokenize(text)

Before a computer can work with text, it must break it into individual, meaningful pieces called tokens. Our tokenize function does three things in sequence:

function tokenize(text) {
  return text
    .toLowerCase()               // 1. normalise case
    .replace(/[^a-z\s]/g, "")   // 2. strip punctuation
    .split(/\s+/)              // 3. split into words
    .filter(w => w.length > 1 && !stopWords.has(w));
                                 // 4. remove stop words
}
Why this matters

"Pizza" and "pizza" are the same word to us, but different strings to a computer. Lowercasing ensures they match. Words like "the", "is", "and" appear in every document — they carry no useful meaning for retrieval, so we filter them out. What remains are the signal words that distinguish one document from another.

Live example

Input: "I love pizza and tacos. Pizza is my favorite food."

Step 2 — Vectorize vectorize(text, vocab)

Computers can't compare words directly. We need to convert each document into a list of numbers — a vector. The idea is simple: create one slot for every word in the entire vocabulary, then count how many times each word appears.

function vectorize(text, vocab) {
  const tokens = tokenize(text);
  return vocab.map(word =>
    tokens.filter(t => t === word).length
  );
}
// vocab: ["amazing", "app", "basketball", "bought", ...]
// output: [ 0, 0, 0, 0, ...]
// one number per vocabulary word
Why this matters

This is called a bag-of-words representation. We lose word order ("pizza love" and "love pizza" produce identical vectors) but gain something powerful: every document now lives in a numerical space where we can use maths to measure similarity. Real-world systems use embeddings from neural networks instead of word counts — but the principle is identical: text in, numbers out.

Document vectors (non-zero cells highlighted)

Step 3 — Compare Vectors cosineSimilarity(a, b)

Now that documents are numbers, we need a way to measure how similar two vectors are. Cosine similarity measures the angle between them — not their length.

function cosineSimilarity(a, b) {
  const dot  = a.reduce((sum, ai, i) => sum + ai * b[i], 0);
  const magA = Math.sqrt(a.reduce((s, v) => s + v*v, 0));
  const magB = Math.sqrt(b.reduce((s, v) => s + v*v, 0));
  return magA && magB ? dot / (magA * magB) : 0;
}
// Returns 0.0 (completely different) to 1.0 (identical)

Why cosine, not distance?

A short document and a long document about the same topic have different vector magnitudes (one has bigger numbers). Euclidean distance would say they're far apart. Cosine similarity ignores magnitude and looks only at direction — if two vectors point the same way, the documents discuss the same things.

The three parts

Dot product — multiply matching slots and sum. High when vectors share the same non-zero positions.
Magnitude — the "length" of each vector.
Division — normalises the score to 0–1.

Similarity matrix — all documents compared

Step 4 — Retrieve retrieve(query, index, topK)

This is the "R" in RAG. The user's question is vectorized, compared against every indexed document, and the top-K most similar documents are returned.

function retrieve(query, index, topK = 2) {
  const qVec = vectorize(query);
  return index
    .map(doc => ({
      ...doc,
      score: cosineSimilarity(qVec, doc.vector)
    }))
    .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score)
    .slice(0, topK);
}
Why this matters

An LLM has a limited context window — it can only read so much text at once. We can't dump every document into the prompt. Retrieval lets us select only the relevant ones. The parameter topK controls how many we include: too few and we miss context, too many and we waste space or confuse the model. In production, vector databases like Pinecone or pgvector make this step fast even with millions of documents.

Try it — type a query and see retrieval scores

Step 5 — Augment the Prompt buildPrompt(question, docs)

This is the "A" in RAG. We take the retrieved documents and inject them into a carefully structured prompt. The LLM now has specific, relevant knowledge it wouldn't otherwise have.

function buildPrompt(question, docs) {
  const context = docs.map(d => d.text).join("\n");

  return `Answer based ONLY on the context below.

  Context:
  ${context}

  Question: ${question}
  Answer:`;
}

Without RAG

The LLM sees only: "What food should I eat for dinner?"

It must rely entirely on its training data — which may be outdated, generic, or hallucinated.

With RAG

The LLM sees the question plus retrieved context:

"Rice and chicken are great for dinner…"
"I love pizza and tacos…"

Now it can ground its answer in your actual data.

Why "ONLY on the context below"?

This instruction constrains the LLM to answer from the retrieved documents, not from its general training. This reduces hallucination — the model is less likely to invent facts when explicitly told to stick to the provided context. It's a form of grounding.

The augmented prompt (after retrieval)
Enter a query in Step 4 to see the prompt here…

Step 6 — Generate the "G" in RAG

The augmented prompt is sent to the LLM, which generates an answer grounded in the retrieved context. In our demo we simulate this; in production you'd call a model API.

// Production code:
const answer = await llm.complete(prompt);

// The LLM generates an answer that references
// the specific documents we retrieved.
// Without RAG: generic / hallucinated answer.
// With RAG: grounded, accurate, verifiable answer.

Putting It All Together

1

tokenize() — Breaks text into meaningful words. Normalises case, strips punctuation, removes stop words. Turns messy human text into clean tokens.

2

vectorize() — Converts tokens into a numeric vector by counting word frequencies. This is how text enters "math space" where similarity can be computed.

3

cosineSimilarity() — Measures the angle between two vectors. Finds documents that discuss similar topics regardless of document length.

4

retrieve() — Vectorizes the user's query, compares it against all indexed documents, and returns the top-K most relevant matches.

5

buildPrompt() — Injects the retrieved context into a structured prompt, grounding the LLM's answer in real data and reducing hallucination.

Try the full interactive demo — Visit /demos/rag in this application to upload your own documents, watch them get tokenized, vectorized and indexed live, then query them with the complete RAG pipeline.

Foundations

Defining the AI Agent

An agent is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon that environment through actuators. — Russell & Norvig

🌍

Environment

The world the agent interacts with.

👁

Sensors

Observe the environment (text, images, audio).

🔧

Actuators

Tools to interact with and change the environment.

🧠

Effector

The "brain" — observations become actions.

Figure 1-1 — Effector, Sensors, Actuators, Environment

In practice: Effector = reasoning LLM, Actuators = tools, Sensors = multimodal inputs, User = Environment.

Figure 1-2 — User ↔ Reasoning LLM (Memory, Tools, Planning) ↔ Environment

The Brain: How LLMs Work

An LLM predicts the next token given input text. Through autoregression it builds complete answers.

From Prediction to Reasoning

Let models "think out loud" — generate reasoning traces before the final answer.

Regular LLM

"Thinks quietly." Outputs only the answer.

Reasoning LLM

"Thinks out loud." Generates traces, then answers.

LLMs are stateless. Memory modules give them continuity across conversations.
